name: ECB Guide on Climate-Related and Environmental Risks
focus: Supervisory expectations for the prudential management and disclosure of climate-related and environmental risks by ECB-supervised institutions
reference_documents: ECB Guide on climate-related and environmental risks (November 2020)
ECB supervisory expectations on climate and environmental risk management (2020-2024)
ECB thematic review on climate-related and environmental risks (2022)

applicability.scope: Significant institutions (SIs) directly supervised by the ECB; also serves as reference for less significant institutions (LSIs) supervised by national competent authorities

expectations.name: Understanding of Climate-Related and Environmental Risks
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to understand the impact of climate-related and environmental risks on the business environment they operate in, in the short, medium and long term, to be able to make informed strategic and business decisions.

expectations.name: Business Environment Monitoring
expectations.description: When determining and implementing their business strategy, institutions are expected to integrate climate-related and environmental risks that impact their business environment in the short, medium and long term.

expectations.name: Business Strategy Integration
expectations.description: The management body is expected to consider climate-related and environmental risks when developing the institution's overall business strategy, business objectives and risk management framework, and to exercise effective oversight of climate-related and environmental risks.

expectations.name: Risk Appetite Framework
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to explicitly include climate-related and environmental risks in their risk appetite framework.

expectations.name: Organisational Structure and Responsibilities
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to assign responsibility for the management of climate-related and environmental risks within the organisational structure in accordance with the three lines of defence model.

expectations.name: Internal Reporting and Risk Data Aggregation
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to report aggregated risk data that reflect their climate-related and environmental risk exposures, enabling the management body and relevant sub-committees to make informed decisions.

expectations.name: Risk Management Framework
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to incorporate climate-related and environmental risks as drivers of existing prudential risk categories into their existing risk management framework, with a view to managing, monitoring and mitigating these over a sufficiently long-term horizon.

expectations.name: Credit Risk Management
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to consider climate-related and environmental risks at all relevant stages of the credit-granting process and to monitor the risks in their portfolios.

expectations.name: Operational and Reputational Risk
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to consider how climate-related and environmental events could have an adverse impact on business continuity and the extent to which the nature of their activities could increase reputational and liability risks.

expectations.name: Market Risk and Investment
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to monitor, on an ongoing basis, the effect of climate-related and environmental factors on their current market risk positions and future investments, and to develop stress-testing scenarios incorporating climate-related and environmental risks.

expectations.name: Liquidity Risk
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to assess whether material climate-related and environmental risks could cause net cash outflows or depletion of liquidity buffers and, if so, incorporate these factors into their liquidity risk management and liquidity buffer calibration.

expectations.name: Disclosure Policies and Procedures
expectations.description: For the purposes of their regulatory disclosures, institutions are expected to publish meaningful information and key metrics on climate-related and environmental risks that they deem to be material, with due regard to EU non-financial reporting guidelines.

expectations.name: Key Metrics and Vulnerabilities Disclosure
expectations.description: Institutions are expected to disclose meaningful information and key metrics on climate-related and environmental risks with reference to the institution's key areas of vulnerability to such risks, including transition and physical risk exposures.

risk_categories.name: Transition Risk
risk_categories.description: Risks arising from the process of adjustment towards a lower-carbon economy, including policy, legal, technology and market changes

risk_categories.name: Physical Risk
risk_categories.description: Risks arising from the physical effects of climate change, including acute events and chronic shifts

risk_categories.name: Credit Risk (C&E driver)
risk_categories.description: Climate and environmental risks as drivers of credit risk through counterparty exposure

risk_categories.name: Market Risk (C&E driver)
risk_categories.description: Climate and environmental risks as drivers of market risk through asset repricing

risk_categories.name: Operational Risk (C&E driver)
risk_categories.description: Climate and environmental risks as drivers of operational risk including business continuity

risk_categories.name: Liquidity Risk (C&E driver)
risk_categories.description: Climate and environmental risks as drivers of liquidity risk through funding and buffer impacts

risk_categories.name: Reputational Risk (C&E driver)
risk_categories.description: Climate and environmental risks as drivers of reputational risk through greenwashing or inaction

related_frameworks: EBA Guidelines on ESG risks management
supervisory_context.assessment_approach: The ECB assesses compliance through the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP), thematic reviews, and on-site inspections. Findings feed into Pillar 2 requirements and qualitative supervisory measures.
supervisory_context.note: Specific supervisory sub-criteria and detailed assessment grids used internally by the ECB may not be publicly available. The expectations documented here reflect the published ECB Guide and related public communications. Some granular assessment details may require expert review against the latest ECB communications.
